text: Up to 60% of female athletes experience iron deficiency.
Iron deficiency negatively affects endurance performance by 3%-4% in high-level female athletes.
population: high-level female athletes (VO2max > 45 mL/kg/min or trained > 5 h/week)

text: Iron-deficient female athletes can improve endurance performance by 2%-20% with oral supplementation of 100 mg/day elemental iron for up to 56 days, or bi-daily parenteral administration over 8-10 days.
specific_value: 2%-20% improvement, 100 mg/day oral for ≤56 days or bi-daily parenteral for 8-10 days
population: high-level iron-deficient female athletes (serum ferritin < 40 µg/L)

text: Iron-deficient non-anemic female athletes with low serum ferritin stores may have reduced maximal aerobic capacity, which can improve by 6%-15% with 16-100 mg/day elemental iron supplementation over 36-126 days.
Iron deficiency in female athletes is defined as serum ferritin below 40 µg/L for performance-relevant thresholds.
Isokinetic strength and anaerobic power may be impeded by up to 23% in iron-deficient female athletes, though the effect of iron supplementation on anaerobic power is highly variable (-5% to +9%).
specific_value: -23% to +4% deficit; -5% to +9% response to supplementation

text: The severity of iron deficiency correlates with the magnitude of performance decrements across endurance, strength, and anaerobic power in female athletes.
